answer:
**The precise Sanskrit term "ekam" (the One, as in the singular ultimate reality) does not appear in the Old Javanese (Kawi) *tattva* texts available in the Falsafa corpus.**[^1] These texts—*San Hyan Tattvajnana*, *San Hyan Mahajnana*, *Gaṇapatitattva*, and *Vṛhaspatitattva*—instead articulate the concept of supreme unity through the highest *tattva*s (principles of reality), particularly *Paramāśivatattva* (Supreme Shiva-principle), which is described as the singular, formless, all-pervading, eternal reality beyond manifestation (*niṣkala*). This aligns with Shaiva Siddhanta's non-dual absolute, from which all else (*cetana* consciousness and *acetana* unconsciousness) emerges and into which it dissolves.

### Core Concept in *San Hyan Tattvajnana*
The text systematically enumerates the *tattva*s, beginning with *Śivatattva* (conscious principle) and *Māyātattva* (unconscious principle), but elevates *Paramāśivatattva* as the supreme unity:
- It is motionless, changeless, without beginning or end, filling and pervading the entire cosmos (seven upper and lower worlds) without increase or diminution.[^2]
- Beyond time, action, good/evil, it is "stable, silent, still, and eternal," the foundational *One* underlying multiplicity.[^3]
Lower *tattva*s like *Sadāśivatattva* (active, omniscient creator) and *Ātmikatattva* (individual soul, *ūtaprota*—interwoven yet distinct) derive from this unity, which remains transcendent: "The Divine Sun is transcendent over all... He is one, yet active within."[^4] Ultimate realization reunites the divided self in this *One*: "The one self is what has divisions."[^5]

### Parallels in *San Hyan Mahajnana*
Here, the "great *tattva*" (highest principle) is the singular *Oṃkāra*-Shiva, beyond states (*jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, *tūrya*), unifying all: "Here is The Sacred Supreme Wisdom, the great *tattva*. It is the highest of *tattvas*... Shiva is situated in the ātma-liṅga, and in the void within the void."[^6] Multiplicity (*prakṛti* as "night," *puruṣa* as "sun") dissolves into this *One* via *jñāna*.[^7]

### Echoes in *Gaṇapatitattva*
Unity manifests in *Praṇava* (*Oṃkāra*) knowledge: "Concentration is the oneness of the knowledge of Praṇava; it is the unification of the mind."[^8] The five Selves (Brahmā–Sadāśiva) emerge from the singular *bindu* (point), returning to the Void-One: "The vanishing of the Void is a return to the subtle form... to the Essence of the Unmanifest."[^9]

### Absence in *Vṛhaspatitattva*
No direct equivalent; it parallels the others thematically (e.g., *ātmā* functions), but wiki scan shows no explicit "One."[^10]

Across these texts, "the One" is not a static monad but a dynamic absolute (*Paramāśiva*), source of 36 *tattva*s, realized via *yoga*, *jñāna*, and *Oṃkāra*-meditation for liberation (*mokṣa*). Multiplicity is illusory (*māyā*), dissolving into this pervasive unity. The corpus (37 works) lacks broader Advaita texts like *Īśvarapratyabhijñā*, so coverage is Shaiva-*tattva*-centric.

[^1]: Confirmed via `search_corpus("ekam")` (0 results); broader "eka/ekam/One/oneness" yields tangential hits outside *tattva* texts.[Full chapter citations below.]

[^2]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, Ch. 1, [para. p-34f807](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-34f807).

[^3]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, Ch. 1, [para. p-34f807](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-34f807).

[^4]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, Ch. 1, [para. p-b46534](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-b46534).

[^5]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, Ch. 1, [para. p-b88d47](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-b88d47).

[^6]: *San Hyan Mahajnana*, Ch. 1, [para. p-f7e6ce](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/translation/#p-f7e6ce).

[^7]: *San Hyan Mahajnana*, Ch. 1, [paras. p-5c91e6–p-f7e6ce](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/translation/#p-5c91e6).

[^8]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Ch. 1, [para. p-555b86](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-555b86).

[^9]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Ch. 1, [para. p-f4abfa](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-f4abfa).

[^10]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, Ch. 1 ([full chapter](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/)); wiki confirms no unique "One" phrasing.
